Which of the following functions can describe a traveling wave? (Hint: check if it satisfies the wave equation \(\frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{v^2} \frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial t^2}\))

a) \(y(x,t) = A \cos(kx^2 - \omega t)\)

b) \(y(x,t) = A(x-vt)^2\)

c) \(y(x,t) = A \log(x+vt)\)

docs.title: 10. Resonance
docs.text: Determine the frequency of a standing wave with two antinodes on a guitar string, given the wave velocity is 1500 m/s and the string length is 1.0 m.

docs.title: 11. Animation: Wave Sources
docs.text:
Write an HTML animation in which it is possible to place dots that will serve as sources of waves described by the equation:

\[ u(\vec{r},t) = \frac{A}{|\vec{r}-\vec{r_0}|^\alpha} \sin(k |\vec{r} - \vec{r_0}| - \omega t) \]

where \(\vec{r_0}\) is the position of the dot, and \(\alpha\) is a parameter that can be set within the range \([0, 2]\). The animation should show the superposition of waves from all dots.

docs.title: 12. Animation: Two-Slit Interference
docs.text:
Write an HTML animation simulating Young's experiment, in which two slits act as point sources of coherent waves. The displacement of the resultant wave is the sum of partial waves described by the formula:

\[ u(\vec{r},t) = \frac{A}{|\vec{r}-\vec{r_1}|} \sin(k |\vec{r} - \vec{r_1}| - \omega t) + \frac{A}{|\vec{r}-\vec{r_2}|} \sin(k |\vec{r} - \vec{r_2}| - \omega t) \]

where \(\vec{r_1}\) and \(\vec{r_2}\) are the position vectors of the slits. The user should be able to change the distance between the slits \(d = |\vec{r_1} - \vec{r_2}|\) and the wavelength \(\lambda\). The animation should visualize the resulting interference pattern in real time.

docs.title: 13. Animation: Huygens' Principle
docs.text:
Create an interactive HTML/JavaScript animation visualizing wave propagation in a discrete medium. The workspace should be filled with a grid of points ("atoms") that can transmit vibrations to their neighbors.

Implement a mechanism where each excited point becomes a source of a new elementary (secondary) wave. The animation should allow for:

  • Point excitation: clicking on a single atom triggers a propagating spherical wave.
  • Linear excitation: simultaneously activating a row of atoms to observe how the summation of many elementary waves creates a plane wave front (envelope).
  • Grid density regulation: a slider allowing the user to change the density of atoms to verify how it affects the continuity and quality of the visible wave front.

The goal is to visually demonstrate how the superposition of partial waves forms a macroscopic wave front.

docs.title: 14. Complex Systems I
docs.text:
A block of mass \(m\) is located between two rigid walls, the distance between which is \(L\). The block is connected to the left wall by a spring with a spring constant \(k_1\), and to the right wall by a spring with a constant \(k_2\). Both springs lie on the same axis, have the same natural length \(l_0\), and the condition \(L > 2l_0\) means that they are pre-tensioned. The width of the block is negligible.

At a certain moment, the resting block was given a velocity \(v\). Find the dependence of the block's position on time if it moves without friction along the axis connecting the walls.

docs.title: 15. Complex Systems II
docs.text:
A small ball of mass \(m\) moves without friction along a vertical rod \(AA'\). Two identical springs with a natural length \(l_0\) and spring constant \(k\) are attached to the ball. The other ends of the springs are anchored at fixed points located symmetrically on opposite sides of the rod (at the same height).

The distance of the rod from each of the spring attachment points is \(a\) (where \(a > l_0\)). Neglecting gravitational interaction, in the harmonic approximation, find the frequency of the ball's oscillations along the rod.

docs.title: 16. Complex Systems III
docs.text:
Two balls of mass \(m\) move without friction along two parallel vertical rods \(AA'\) and \(BB'\). These balls are connected in series by springs to two fixed vertical walls and to each other.

The system looks horizontally as follows: wall – spring – rod \(AA'\) (with ball) – spring – rod \(BB'\) (with ball) – spring – wall.

All three springs are identical: they have a spring constant \(k\) and a natural length \(l_0\). The distance between the wall and the rod, as well as between the rods themselves, is \(a\), with the springs being pre-tensioned (\(a > l_0\)).

In the harmonic approximation, find the frequency and normal modes of (vertical) oscillations of this system.

docs.title: 17. Complex Systems IV
docs.text:
Two balls of mass \(m\) move without friction along a single horizontal rod \(AA'\). The system is bounded on both sides by fixed walls.

The balls are connected by springs in the following linear order: left wall – spring – first ball – spring – second ball – spring – right wall.

All springs have the same spring constant \(k\). In the harmonic approximation, find the frequencies and normal modes of (longitudinal) oscillations of the system. Compare the obtained result with the result of problem II.4.

docs.title: 1. Coulomb's Law
docs.text: Four point charges of +1.0 C each are placed at the corners of a square with sides of 1.0 m. Calculate the magnitude and direction of the electric force on a charge of -2.0 C placed at the center of the square.

docs.title: 2. Electric Potential
docs.text: Point charges of +1C, -2C, +3C, and -4C are placed at the corners of a square with sides of 1.0 m (in order). Calculate the electric potential at the center of the square.

docs.title: 3. Electrostatic Equilibrium
docs.text: Find the equilibrium position for a charge \(q_3 = +1\text{C}\) placed on the line between a charge \(q_1 = +4\text{C}\) and a charge \(q_2 = +9\text{C}\), which are separated by a distance of 2 m.

docs.title: 4. Force Comparison
docs.text: Calculate the magnitude of the electric force and the gravitational force between an electron and a proton in a hydrogen atom (average distance \(r \approx 5.3 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}\)). What is the ratio \(F_e/F_g\)?

docs.title: 5. Field Levitation
docs.text: What electric field strength is required to make a proton levitate against Earth's gravity at the level of the Earth's surface? (Mass of proton \(m_p \approx 1.67 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}\), charge of proton \(e \approx 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}\), gravitational acceleration \(g \approx 9.8 \text{ m/s}^2\)).

docs.title: 6. Cyclotron Motion
docs.text: An electron is accelerated from rest through a potential difference of 5000 V. It then enters a region of uniform magnetic field B = 0.1 T, perpendicular to its velocity. What is the radius of the circular path it will follow?

docs.title: 7. Lorentz Force
docs.text: A charged particle with charge \(q = 2 \times 10^{-19}\) C and mass \(m = 4 \times 10^{-27}\) kg enters a magnetic field of \(B = 0.5\) T at a speed of \(v = 10^6\) m/s perpendicular to the field. What is the magnitude of the Lorentz force acting on the particle?

docs.title: 8. Vector Lorentz Force
docs.text: A proton moves with a velocity \(\vec{v} = (2\hat{i} - 4\hat{j} + \hat{k}) \text{ m/s}\) in a region where the magnetic field is \(\vec{B} = (\hat{i} + 2\hat{j} - \hat{k}) \text{ T}\). What is the magnitude of the magnetic force this charge experiences?

docs.title: 9. Lorentz Force on Wire
docs.text:
A straight wire 2.0 m long carries a current of 10 A. It is placed in a uniform magnetic field of B = 0.5 T. Calculate the magnetic force on the wire if the angle between the wire and the magnetic field is:

a) \(90^\circ\) b) \(45^\circ\) c) \(0^\circ\)

docs.title: 10. Mass Spectrometry
docs.text: A proton (charge \(+e\), mass \(m_p\)) and an alpha particle (charge \(+2e\), mass \(\approx 4m_p\)) enter a uniform magnetic field with the same velocity, perpendicular to the field lines. What is the ratio of the radii of their circular paths, \(r_p / r_\alpha\)?

docs.title: 1. Gauss's Law
docs.text: A point charge of \(+2 \text{ C}\) is located at the origin. Calculate the electric flux through a spherical surface of radius \(1 \text{ m}\) centered at the origin.

docs.title: 2. Ampere's Law
docs.text: Two long, parallel wires are \(10 \text{ cm}\) apart and carry currents of \(5 \text{ A}\) in opposite directions. Calculate the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field at a point midway between the wires.

docs.title: 3. Biot-Savart Law
docs.text: A small segment of wire of length \(0.1 \text{ m}\) carries a current of \(3 \text{ A}\). The segment is located at a distance of \(0.2 \text{ m}\) from a point \(P\). Calculate the magnetic field at point \(P\) due to this current segment (assume the segment is perpendicular to the line connecting it to point \(P\)).

docs.title: 4. Magnetic Torque
docs.text: A rectangular loop of wire with dimensions \(10 \text{ cm}\) by \(5 \text{ cm}\) carries a current of \(2 \text{ A}\). A uniform magnetic field of \(B = 0.3 \text{ T}\) is applied parallel to the plane of the loop. What is the magnitude of the magnetic torque on the loop?

docs.title: 5. EM Wave Analysis
docs.text: An electromagnetic wave has its electric field component described by \(E_y(x,t) = 100 \sin(10^7 x - \omega t) \text{ V/m}\). What is the direction of propagation? What is the wavelength \(\lambda\)? What is the angular frequency \(\omega\)? What is the equation for the magnetic field component?

docs.title: 6. Wavelength and Frequency
docs.text: The human eye is most sensitive to light with a wavelength of about \(550 \text{ nm}\). What color does this correspond to in the visible spectrum? What is the frequency of this light?

docs.title: 7. EM Spectrum
docs.text: List the following types of electromagnetic radiation in order of increasing wavelength: Infrared, Ultraviolet, Microwaves, X-rays, Radio waves, Gamma rays.

docs.title: 8. Refraction (Snell's Law)
docs.text: A light ray travels from air (\(n=1.00\)) into glass (\(n=1.50\)). If the angle of incidence is \(30^\circ\), what is the angle of refraction?

docs.title: 9. Speed of Light in Media
docs.text: What is the speed of light in a diamond, which has an index of refraction \(n = 2.42\)?

docs.title: 10. Brewster Angle
docs.text: Light in air strikes a glass surface with refractive index \(n = 1.50\). At what angle of incidence does reflected light become fully polarized?

docs.title: 1. Series and Parallel Circuit
docs.text:
You have three resistors, \(R_1=15\,\Omega\), \(R_2=30\,\Omega\), and \(R_3=50\,\Omega\) and a 12 V battery.

a) Draw the circuit diagram.

b) Calculate the total equivalent resistance.

c) Calculate the current flowing from the battery.

Perform parts a), b), and c) separately for purely series and purely parallel configurations of the three resistors.

docs.title: 2. Mixed Circuit
docs.text: A resistor \(R_1=10\,\Omega\) is connected in series with a parallel combination of two other resistors, \(R_2=20\,\Omega\) and \(R_3=20\,\Omega\). The entire circuit is connected to a 6 V source. What is the total current drawn from the source?

docs.title: 3. Resistors
docs.text: You have a supply of exactly three \(1\,\Omega\) resistors. What are all the possible equivalent resistances you can create by combining them? List all unique values and draw the corresponding circuit diagrams.

docs.title: 4. Resistors again
docs.text: You have some supply of \(1\,\Omega\) resistors. How can you combine them to create an equivalent resistance of \(2.5\,\Omega\)?

docs.title: 5. Kirchhoff's Laws
docs.text: Using Kirchhoff's laws, find the currents \(I_1, I_2, I_3\) in a circuit with two loops. Loop 1 (left): a 10V battery and a \(2\Omega\) resistor, shared branch in the middle. Loop 2 (right): a 5V battery and a \(3\Omega\) resistor. The shared branch has a \(5\Omega\) resistor. Assume currents are flowing out of the positive terminals.

docs.title: 6. Kirchhoff's Laws again
docs.text: For a circuit consisting of a 2V battery and a 1-ohm resistor in the left loop, and a 1V battery and a 3-ohm resistor in the right loop, with a 2-ohm resistor in the middle branch, calculate all currents.

docs.title: 7. Capacitors in Parallel
docs.text: Two capacitors, \(C_1=4\,\mu\text{F}\) and \(C_2=6\,\mu\text{F}\), are connected in parallel to a 10 V battery. What is the total charge stored on the capacitors? What is the total energy stored?

docs.title: 8. Capacitor mixed
docs.text: Find the equivalent capacitance for a circuit where a \(10\,\mu \text{F}\) capacitor is in series with a parallel combination of a \(5\,\mu \text{F}\) capacitor and a \(20\,\mu \text{F}\) capacitor.

docs.title: 9. Charge Storage
docs.text: How much charge is stored on a \(100\,\mu\text{F}\) capacitor when it is connected to a 20 V source?

docs.title: 10. AC Voltage Equation
docs.text: The current in an AC circuit is given by \(I(t) = 2 \sin(120\pi t)\). If the circuit consists of a single \(50\,\Omega\) resistor, what is the equation for the voltage \(V(t)\) across it?

docs.title: 11. Average Current
docs.text: A lightning bolt transfers a charge of 30 Coulombs to the ground in a time of 2 milliseconds. What is the average current of the lightning bolt?

docs.title: 12. Current
docs.text: Charge flowing through the wire is given by the function of time \(Q(t) = 5t^2+5\). What is the current at \(t=3\) s?

docs.title: 13. Power & Energy
docs.text: What is the power dissipated by a \(100\,\Omega\) resistor when a voltage of 50 V is applied across it? How much energy is consumed in 5 minutes?

docs.title: 14. Transformer Currents
docs.text: A transformer has a primary coil with 1000 turns and a secondary coil with 200 turns. If the primary voltage is \(120\text{ V}\) (AC), what is the secondary voltage? If the current in the secondary is \(3\text{ A}\), what is the current in the primary?

docs.title: 15. Transformer Ratio
docs.text: A transformer is used to step down the voltage from 120 V AC to 9.0 V AC. If the primary coil has 400 turns, how many turns must the secondary coil have?

docs.title: 16. RLC Circuit
docs.text: Write down the differential equation for a series RLC circuit with a voltage source \(V\), a resistor \(R\), an inductor \(L\), and a capacitor \(C\). Assume the current is \(I(t)\) and the voltage across the capacitor is \(V_C(t)\). Compare this to the equation of a damped harmonic oscillator. What are the analogies between the terms in the two equations?

docs.title: 17. RLC Impedance
docs.text:
A series RLC circuit has \(R=20\,\Omega\), \(L=10 \text{ mH}\), and \(C=50\,\mu\text{F}\). It is connected to an AC source with a frequency of 100 Hz.

a) Calculate the inductive reactance (\(X_L\)).

b) Calculate the capacitive reactance (\(X_C\)).

c) Calculate the total impedance (\(Z\)) of the circuit.

docs.title: 18. Resonance
docs.text: For the RLC circuit in the previous problem, what is the resonant frequency? What would the impedance of the circuit be at resonance?

docs.title: 19. Resistor Cube
docs.text: A cube is constructed from 12 identical resistors on its edges, each with resistance R. What is the equivalent resistance between two opposite corners of the cube?

docs.title: 1. Propagation of Error I
docs.text: The radius of a sphere is measured to be \(r = (6.20 \pm 0.05)\text{ cm}\). Calculate the volume of the sphere and its associated uncertainty.

docs.title: 2. Propagation of Error II
docs.text: The length and width of a rectangular plate are measured to be \(L = (15.3 \pm 0.1)\text{ cm}\) and \(W = (8.4 \pm 0.1)\text{ cm}\). Calculate the area of the plate and its uncertainty.

docs.title: 3. Propagation of Error III
docs.text: The resistance R is calculated using Ohm's Law, \(R = V/I\). If the voltage is measured as \(V = (10.0 \pm 0.2)\text{ V}\) and the current as \(I = (2.00 \pm 0.05)\text{ A}\), what is the calculated resistance and its uncertainty?

docs.title: 4. Relative Uncertainty
docs.text: A car's speedometer has a 5% of uncertainty. If it reads 60 km/h, what is the range of the car's actual speed?

docs.title: 5. Percentage Calculation
docs.text: A measurement of time is recorded as \(t = 5.45 \pm 0.22\) seconds. What is the percentage uncertainty of this measurement?

docs.title: 6. Instrument Precision
docs.text: A digital thermometer reads \(25.4^\circ\text{C}\). Assuming the uncertainty is half the value of the last digit, what is the absolute uncertainty of this measurement?

docs.title: 7. Standard Deviation
docs.text: Eleven students received the following scores on a test: 88, 92, 79, 85, 95, 81, 86, 90, 83, 77, 89. What is the standard deviation of these test scores?

docs.title: 8. Light Speed Measurement
docs.text: Measure the speed of light using a microwave oven, a bar of chocolate, and a ruler. Measure the distance between the melted spots on the chocolate to determine the wavelength of the microwaves. Use the frequency of the microwave oven, 2.45 GHz, to calculate the speed of light.

docs.title: 9. Pendulum Measurements - html/real
docs.text:
Create a simple pendulum simulator in HTML equipped with a stopwatch for manual timing. Assuming the string length is an exact value, run the simulation and perform 10 measurements of the time taken for 10 complete oscillations. Based on the obtained results, manually calculate the mean period and standard deviation. Using this data, determine the value of the acceleration due to gravity and calculate the measurement uncertainty of this result.

Repeat the real-life experiment: set up a physical pendulum using a string and a small mass (e.g., a metal ball, keychain, necklace). Measure the length of the pendulum and perform 10 measurements of the time taken for 10 complete oscillations using a stopwatch in your cellphone. Calculate the mean period and its standard deviation. Use this data to calculate the value of the acceleration due to gravity and its associated uncertainty.

docs.title: 10. Mass-Spring Measurements - html
docs.text: Generate a simulator of a mass suspended on a spring in HTML with a timing function. Treating the mass as a given value with zero uncertainty, perform a series of 10 time measurements for 10 complete oscillations. Use the collected data to calculate the mean period, standard deviation, and Type A uncertainty. Calculate the value of the spring constant along with its measurement uncertainty.

docs.title: 1. Scale Comparison
docs.text: How many times farther is the Sun from Earth compared to the Moon from Earth? (Sun-Earth distance \(\approx 150 \times 10^6\) km; Moon-Earth distance \(\approx 384,000\) km).

docs.title: 2. Angular Size
docs.text: Calculate the angular size (in degrees) of the Sun and the Moon as seen from Earth. Sun's diameter \(\approx 1.39 \times 10^6\) km; Moon's diameter \(\approx 3,474\) km. Why do they appear to be roughly the same size? If the Moon were the size of a basketball (about 24 cm in diameter), how big would the Sun be in this scale model? (Sun's diameter \(\approx 1.39 \times 10^6\) km).

docs.title: 3. Rotational Velocity
docs.text: Calculate the linear speed (in km/s) of a point on the Earth's equator due to its rotation. Earth's radius \(\approx 6378\) km.

docs.title: 4. Orbital Mechanics
docs.text: Calculate the orbital speed of the International Space Station (ISS), which orbits at an altitude of approximately 400 km above the Earth's surface. (Earth's mass \(M_E \approx 5.97 \times 10^{24}\) kg). Compare this speed of Earth's orbital speed around the Sun (Earth-Sun distance \(\approx 150 \times 10^6\) km, Earth's orbital period \(\approx 365.25\) days). Which is faster, the ISS around the Earth or Earth in its orbit around the Sun?

docs.title: 5. Microgravity
docs.text: What is the acceleration due to gravity (\(g\)) at the altitude of the ISS (400 km)? Why do astronauts experience a state of "weightlessness" despite this gravity?

docs.title: 6. Geostationary Orbit
docs.text: Satellites in geostationary orbit remain above the same point on Earth. What must their orbital period be? Calculate the altitude of a geostationary orbit above the Earth's surface.

docs.title: 7. Escape Velocity
docs.text: What is the escape velocity from the surface of the Moon? (Moon's mass \(M_M \approx 7.35 \times 10^{22}\) kg; whereas Moon's radius \(R_M \approx 1,737\) km). Express the result in km/s and as a fraction of Earth's escape velocity (Earth's escape velocity \(\approx 11.2\) km/s).

docs.title: 8. Solar Gravity
docs.text: Calculate the acceleration due to gravity on the surface of the Sun. By what factor would your weight increase if you could stand on its surface? (Sun's mass \(M_S \approx 2 \times 10^{30}\) kg; Sun's radius \(R_S \approx 6.96 \times 10^8\) m).

docs.title: 9. Megastructures
docs.text: A "Dyson Sphere" is a hypothetical megastructure that completely encompasses a star to capture its energy output. If the mass of Mercury (\(3.3 \times 10^{23}\) kg) were used to build a solar panel sphere with a surface density of 10 kg/m², what would be the radius of the sphere?

docs.title: 10. Interplanetary Travel
docs.text:
How much time would it take to get from Earth to Mars when Mars is at its closest approach (55 million km)

a) for a message sent at the speed of light?

b) by a spacecraft traveling at a constant speed of 40,000 km/h = 11.11 km/s?

c) by "airplane" traveling at a constant speed of 900 km/h, typical for intercontinental flights?

docs.title: 1. Cosmic Chronology
docs.text: Place the following events of the early universe in chronological order (from earliest to latest): Recombination (formation of neutral atoms), Big Bang Nucleosynthesis, Inflation, Formation of first galaxies, Quark-gluon plasma era.

docs.title: 2. Cosmic Temperatures
docs.text: Order temperatures of objects in the universe from hottest to coldest: the core of the Sun, the surface of the Sun, the present Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) radiation, interstellar space, temperature of the Univeres at the nucleosynthesis era, human body temperature, temperature of the Universe at the recombination era, temperature of black hole with 10 solar masses, ionization temperature of hydrogen.

docs.title: 3. Cosmic Sizes
docs.text: Order the following objects in the universe from smallest to largest: Earth, Milky Way galaxy, Solar System, observable universe, Sun, Andromeda galaxy, human, Moon, Pluto, Jupiter, Ceres, Local Group of galaxies

docs.title: 4. Hubble's Law
docs.text: According to Hubble's Law \(v = H_0 d\), and using the value of \(H_0 \approx 70 \text{ (km/s)/Mpc}\), at what distance would a galaxy be receding from us at the speed of light? This defines the approximate size of the observable universe.

docs.title: 4. Galactic Year
docs.text: Our Solar System orbits the center of the Milky Way (a distance of about 27,000 light-years) at a speed of about 220 km/s. How long does it take to complete one orbit (a "galactic year")?

docs.title: 5. Galactic Collision
docs.text: The Andromeda galaxy is about 2.5 million light-years away and is moving towards our Milky Way at about 110 km/s. Estimate how long it will be until the two galaxies collide.

docs.title: 6. Galactic Communication
docs.text: How much time would it take to exchange mutual greetings between two civilizations located at opposite ends of the Milky Way, assuming they can communicate at the speed of light?

docs.title: 7. Galactic Trivia
docs.text:
a) What is the Milky Way galaxy's ratio of diameter to its thickness?

b) If 200 billion stars constituting the Milky Way were distributed equally among the present Earth's people, how many stars would each person get?

c) How much time would it take to count all the stars in the Milky Way if you counted one star per second without stopping?

docs.title: 8. Cosmic Expansion
docs.text: If the universe is expanding at a rate of 70 km/s per megaparsec, how much will the distance between two galaxies that are currently 100 million light-years apart increase after 1 billion years? Assume that the expansion rate remains constant over this time period. (1 Mpc \(\approx 3.26\) million light-years).

docs.title: 9. Doppler Effect
docs.text: A galaxy is receding from Earth at a speed of \(5 \times 10^6 \text{ m/s}\). It emits light with a wavelength of 486 nm (blue-green). What wavelength will be observed on Earth? Assume non-relativistic Doppler shift for light: \(\lambda_{observed} = \lambda_{emitted} \left(1 + \frac{v}{c}\right)\), where \(v\) is the recession speed and \(c\) is the speed of light. Then use the relativistic Doppler shift formula to find the observed wavelength: \(\lambda_{observed} = \lambda_{emitted} \sqrt{\frac{1 + \frac{v}{c}}{1 - \frac{v}{c}}}\), and compare the results.

docs.title: 10. Cosmic Microwave Background
docs.text: The Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) radiation now has a temperature of about 2.7 K. When it was emitted during the recombination era, it had a temperature of about 3000 K. What was the wavelength of the peak intensity of the CMB radiation at the time of recombination, and what is its wavelength now? Use Wien's displacement law: \(\lambda_{peak} = \frac{b}{T}\), where \(b \approx 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m K}\) is Wien's constant.

docs.title: 1. Length Contraction
docs.text: At what speed must a 1-meter-long ruler travel for its length to be observed as 0.5 meters due to length contraction?

docs.title: 2. Time Dilation
docs.text: Two identical devices, each playing a series of 1-hour episodes of a TV show, were built and synchronized on Earth. One device stayed on Earth, while the other was placed on a spaceship that was immediately accelerated to relativistic speed. How fast must the spaceship be moving for the device on Earth to have played 2 episodes while the device on the spaceship has played only 1 episode?

docs.title: 3. Time Dilation in Real Life
docs.text: A muon has an average lifetime of \(2.20\,\mu\text{s}\) in its own rest frame. If it travels at \(0.990c\), what will its average lifetime be as measured by an observer on Earth?

docs.title: 4. High-Energy Physics
docs.text: How much energy (in GeV) is required to accelerate a proton from rest to a speed of \(0.99c\)? The rest mass of a proton is approximately \(938 \text{ MeV/c}^2\).

docs.title: 5. Relativistic Mass
docs.text: What is the relativistic mass of a 50 kg supermodel traveling at 99.9% of the speed of light?

docs.title: 6. Mass-Energy
docs.text: The "Little Boy" atomic bomb converted approximately 0.7 grams of mass into energy. Using \(E=mc^2\), calculate the energy released in Joules. (1 kiloton of TNT is approximately \(4.184 \times 10^{12}\) J).

docs.title: 7. Velocity Transformation
docs.text: A rocket traveling at \(0.8c\) away from Earth shoots a probe forward at \(0.5c\) relative to the rocket. What is the probe's speed as measured by an observer on Earth according to classical mechanics, and what is it according to special relativity?

docs.title: 8. Adding Velocities
docs.text: What would happen to the relative velocities of two objects moving away from each other if one of the objects is moving at the speed of light?

docs.title: 9. Twin Paradox
docs.text: An astronaut spends one year on the International Space Station (ISS), which travels at about \(7.66 \text{ km/s}\). How much younger would the astronaut be than their twin on Earth due to the kinetic time dilation?

docs.title: 10. Gravitational Time Dilation
docs.text: How much younger would the astronaut be than their twin on Earth purely due to gravitational time dilation after one year? Assume the ISS orbits at an altitude of 400 km above Earth's surface.

docs.title: 1. De Broglie Wavelength
docs.text: Calculate the de Broglie wavelength of an electron with a kinetic energy of 150 eV.

docs.title: 2. Macroscopic Limits
docs.text: Calculate the de Broglie wavelength of a 50-gram golf ball traveling at a speed of 60 m/s. Why are quantum effects not noticeable for macroscopic objects?

docs.title: 3. Photon vs Electron
docs.text: What happens to the energy of a photon if its wavelength is doubled? What happens to the kinetic energy of an electron (non-relativistically) if its de Broglie wavelength is doubled?

docs.title: 4. Photoelectric Effect
docs.text: The work function of potassium is 2.3 eV. What is the maximum kinetic energy of electrons ejected from potassium when it is illuminated with ultraviolet light of wavelength 350 nm?

docs.title: 5. Heisenberg Principle
docs.text: Using the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle (\(\Delta x \Delta p \ge \hbar/2\)), what is the minimum uncertainty in the velocity of an electron that is confined within a region of space 0.1 nm wide (the size of an atom)?

docs.title: 6. Emission Spectra
docs.text: Calculate the energy of the photon emitted by a hydrogen atom when an electron transitions from the n=3 state to the n=1 state. What is the wavelength of this photon?

docs.title: 7. Bohr Model
docs.text: Calculate the radius of the second orbit (n=2) in a hydrogen atom using the Bohr model.

docs.title: 8. Angular Momentum
docs.text: What is the angular momentum of an electron in the n=4 state of a hydrogen atom, according to the Bohr model?

docs.title: 9. Threshold Frequency
docs.text: For light, at what frequency will the photoelectric effect begin for a metal with a work function of 4.5 eV?

docs.title: 10. X-Ray Production
docs.text: What is the minimum potential difference that must be applied across an X-ray tube to produce X-rays with a wavelength of \(0.01\) nm? (Hint: \(E = hf = hc/\lambda = qV\)).

docs.title: 1. Quantum Numbers
docs.text: For the \(n=3\) energy level in a hydrogen atom, what are the possible values for the quantum numbers \(l\) (orbital) and \(m_l\) (magnetic)? How many distinct electron states exist for \(n=3\)?

docs.title: 2. Energy Scaling
docs.text:
State the general dependence of energy E on the principal quantum number n (i.e., \(E \propto n^?\)) for:

a) A particle in a one-dimensional infinite potential well.

b) An electron in a hydrogen atom.

docs.title: 3. Photon Energy
docs.text: Calculate the energy (in eV) of a photon with a wavelength of 500 nm. Use the formula \(E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}\) where \(h = 4.1357 \times 10^{-15}\) [eVs] and \(c = 3.0 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}\).

docs.title: 4. Radioactive Half-Life
docs.text: The half-life of Cobalt-60 is 5.27 years. If a sample initially contains 100 grams of Cobalt-60, how much will remain after approximately 21 years?

docs.title: 5. Alpha Decay
docs.text: Give a specific, balanced nuclear equation for an alpha decay process, starting with Uranium-238 (\({}^{238}_{92}\text{U}\)).

docs.title: 6. Beta Decay
docs.text: Give a specific, balanced nuclear equation for a beta-minus decay process, starting with Carbon-14 (\({}^{14}_{6}\text{C}\)).

docs.title: 7. Pair Annihilation
docs.text: An electron and a positron, each with a rest mass of \(0.511 \text{MeV/c}^2\), annihilate each other, producing two photons of equal energy. What is the energy (in MeV) and wavelength of each photon?

docs.title: 8. Quark Model
docs.text: What is the quark composition of a proton and a neutron? Use this to verify their electric charges (u quark charge = +2/3 e, d quark charge = -1/3 e).

docs.title: 9. Quantum Well Energy
docs.text: An electron is in a 1D infinite potential well of width \(L = 0.5\) nm. It is in the \(n=2\) state. What is the energy of the electron in eV?

docs.title: 10. Wavefunction Probability
docs.text: For a particle in a 1D box of length L, the wavefunction for the ground state is \(\Psi(x) = \sqrt{2/L} \sin(\pi x/L)\). Calculate the probability of finding the particle in the region \(0 \le x \le L/4\). Then calculate the probability of finding the particle in the region \(L/4 \le x \le L/2\). Which region is more likely to contain the particle?
